sentences.text: Well, you see that Hungary, as such, was an ally of Germany.
And basically, Hungarians believed that our government is going to protect Hungary, as such, from any major damage.
Although they had a political party which was called the Arrow Cross party.
The Arrow Cross party was absolute Nazi, absolute pro-Hitler, absolute anti-- first of all, very, very anti-Semitic and anti-Jewish.
Now, this party started to come, basically, to the surface, probably around the early 1940s.
Already the war, we knew about Anschluss.
We knew about Germany occupying Austria.
We knew that Germany occupied Czechoslovakia.
But somehow, somewhere, we felt-- beside of my father being a highly-decorated soldier from the First World War-- he was excluded from some basic Jewish laws.
Which basically didn't affect us, but affected him in a-- on a commercial sense.
But the big change, the major changes came as the war was rolling around, in the 1942s and '43s.
And we were completely, completely under the influence of-- and the propaganda of the-- of the-- of the Nazis of the-- the Germans, basically, and the Hungarians.
We had-- my father bought and my brother-in-law installed an antenna.
So we were able to get BBC, which is a British Broadcasting Company, news in Hungarian twice a day, which was 100% against the law.
If my father would have been caught listening to those news, it would have been-- I don't know.
I don't know what was the penalty, but jail for sure.
So it was not allowed to have.
So we had a shortwave radio.
The radio was in our bedroom and in the attic, we installed, in our house, an antenna, my brother and I.
We knew enough, that's how to do it.
During this news, the instruction was that at the time, each time both of us, we had to be outside-- outside and play ball.
And we played ball.
And God forbid anybody came around the house.
At that time, we just had to throw the ball against the window.
And my father knew that meant that he has to switch it off.

And then the-- the-- the big change, the huge change, what I was say that would change, totally, all of us, life, came at the time when the Germans were unsatisfied and unhappy with the cooperation and-- and service, what the Hungarians gave to the war effort.
And March 19, 1944, it was a Sunday.
They came in and they occupied Hungary.
To me and my brother, that was an afternoon where it was locally-- went to a soccer game.
We were at the soccer game, when suddenly we saw the German army, tanks, trucks, motorcycles, cars and this, rolling in.
And naturally, we were horrified.
And I can't imagine what was the-- my mom and dad's feeling what they saw what's happening.
And they knew that we are not around.
But very smartly, my brother, it was a little bit around halftime of the soccer game.
And my brother came to the-- turned to me.
And he says, OK, let's go home.
This is no time to be here.
And we went home.
And there was a tremendous welcome at home from mom and dad.
And we knew that Hungary, at that time, although the radio did not announce anything yet, officially.
You know, at that time there was no television.
So the radio did not announce that there's any occupation.
But we knew what's happening.
I mean, we saw the German army coming through.
By the way, where we lived, that suburb of Budapest was 20 miles west of Budapest, where from Austria and Czechoslovakia, the German army came in, heading towards Budapest.
And so we had the-- let's call it the occupation or the invasion prior to the city of Budapest.
Well, naturally the radio station and the other political center of the city or the center of Hungary was.
And then I don't know if I mentioned to you before right now, actually, I have-- I have three children.
And that afternoon my parents sat down with us.
And although there was a little bit of rejection from my mom.
But the conclusion was that next morning, Monday morning, everybody has to go occupation or no occupation, to go to work.
That's mean, go to the place where you belong.
And that morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a little bit of tears in her's eyes, mom packed our lunch.
We took our school bag Monday morning.
And we went to school.
We had to take-- well, usually it was that we took a train.
It was an express train.
We had the first-- only one stop going into the Budapest.
From there, we took the streetcar.
Now, I mentioned to you that I have three children.

And the reason I'm mentioning it now, that I hope that never in my lifetime or their life-- or their lifetime, they ever have to come to a conclusion of that's importance or that magnitude, what my parents had to do at that time.

But if, God forbid, that would happen, I would have told them, or I would came-- I will try to convince them of the same thing, that regardless what happens, you have to go to where you belong, which was school.
And Monday morning we left.
We took the train.
From the train, we went on the streetcar.
Well, by the way, at that time already, what you have to understand, that a Jew was not really welcomed anywhere.
The system in Hungary at gymnasiums or any kind of middle school or higher school is that every student had to wear a cap.
That cap basically totally identified who you are or not who you are, but which school you belong to.
Without that cap, you couldn't enter the school grounds.
But that cap describes which school you belonged.
My brother had the cap which had the fasces sign, which is similar to the-- I mean, that was the official sign of the Italian government, or the Italian Nazi party, or whatever you want to call it.
From Mussolini and everybody down, wore it.
That was similar to the-- German [GERMAN].
What's-- what's-- what's the-- cross.
But I had to wear my high school's cap which wore-- which identified me as I am belong-- I belong to the Jewish gymnasium, the Jewish high school.
So automatically, everybody knew that I was Jewish.
And sometimes people were just spitting at you or calling you, right in the streetcar, all kind of names.
What-- especially that particular day.
I mean, I was-- we were holding onto each other.
And we worried about only one thing, get to school.
As we get off to the streetcar, Monday morning, it was early in the morning.
Our school started at eight o'clock.
And Hungarian police, gendarmes, and SS guards were where we get off.
We were-- our school were approximately a block and a half, two blocks apart.
So we get off the same-- always on the same streetcars.
Stop.
And they ask the question.
And very interestingly, the question wasn't asked for me.
Well, they asked for my brother, who was wearing the fasces sign, are you Jewish?
And he said, yes.
He says, go against the wall.
Stay against the wall.
And they let me go.
And I insisted hey, that's my brother.
I want to be with him.
And not only the closeness, but suddenly we couldn't-- I couldn't figure out, and brash, 15-and-a-1/2-year-old, you know, who is afraid of nothing and nobody, went and stood with my brother.
And suddenly, people, more and more people were pushed against the wall.
And then probably a couple of hours later, we were marched into the headquarters of the Budapest Police Station.
Which was a big courtyard, it's nothing else but a courtyard, building all around.
And more and more and more and more people came there.

And what I would like to tell you, that Budapest is basically-- or effectively, I think so-- the same geographical height as northern part of the United States, like Buffalo, Syracuse, or whatever you want to call-- Albany, I mean, New York State wise.
It was cold, miserable, rainy.
Well, just a bad day of-- of being outside.
Although you know, like we had our jackets, our coats.
Side We had coats.
Definitely at that time, March, we had coats.
And-- and but we had only our school bag, the school's-- right now it's one-- once again fashionable that you have the bag-- backpack.
At that time, it was the backpack was our school bag, and our lunch.
And we had no idea, but we suddenly realized that everybody in that courtyard was Jewish.
More and more and more and more and more people were pushed in there.
And we stayed there, I know for sure, it was two nights.
But I don't believe it was further than two nights.
And it-- always outside.
We never had the chance to go inside.
Never had a shelter.
Never heard, anybody, any question.
And from that day on, although we heard previously rumors, but from that day on, that rumor, life and live by the rumor was extremely high.
Rumor was basically, that they are assembling people.
And Jews, being arrested, that we are going to go and go to some kind of labor camp, or somehow, somewhere, to work towards the betterment of the war, for the Nazis.
Well, it was-- you know, like-- I-- I-- it's very difficult to describe.
But the-- the major support was my brother who was, at that time, already almost 18.
And he was strong and continuously telling and explaining that you have to do anything, whatsoever, to be strong and support and-- and-- and supportive of each other, number one.
And try to do anything possible to survive.
The only thing, what we tried at that time-- well, naturally we couldn't achieve anything at least, not at the beginning, to let mom and dad know that we are alive, where we are.
I mean, they knew only one thing, that we left for school.
And by the first afternoon, they knew that we are not back anywhere where we're supposed to be.
No.
There was no such a thing.
Later on we found the gendarme who was willing to do it.
Because we promised to that Hungarian gendarme if he delivers the message to-- to them, call-- to call them on the telephone he's going to receive like, let's translate it, $100.
And we had no idea if he's going to do it, yes or not.
Although, the same time, I have to tell you a little anecdote.
Because maybe you-- during that time, my brother met a gentleman who was an uncle of a friend of his classmate, naturally, a Jewish gentleman, who recognized my brother.
And the second-- the first morning, after the first night which was miserable and we were naturally very hungry, especially kids, suddenly he said that he has some food for us.
And these gentlemen opened up the briefcase and from the briefcase they took out raw eggs.
I looked at him.
My brother looked at him now.
Great, you have raw eggs.
Because he told us be careful, they are raw.

And he got a little pocket knife from his pocket-- he was an engineer-- and opened up-- made a little hole on one end of the egg, then made a hole, put his finger and he turn it over, made an egg and the other hole.
Just told me I was the first one.
He said, put your finger on it.
He said, put it to your mouth and suck out the egg.
And I looked at him.
I was a finicky eater, difficult eater.
I said, no way.
And that's what I still feel today.
That my brother slapped me across the face.
And he said, from now on, whatever they give you to eat, if you want to survive, you eat.
And I sucked that egg out.
Till today, don't show me sunny-sides up.
[LAUGHS]
From there-- from the Budapest police quarters, we were taken after-- well, I think it was the third day-- by truck to a internment camp-- camp.
And that was around 25, 30 miles west of Budapest-- southwest of Budapest, called Kistercsa, K-I-S-T-E-R-C-S-A.
And that was-- I assume-- I never heard about this place before.
Naturally, as a kid, I didn't hear about it.
That was an internment camp for prisoners, prisoners who were not ready yet, probably before sentencing.
I have no idea.
It was an internment camp, fully walled and barbed wire and guarded, and guard houses.
And I mean, it was-- was a camp.
Every-- not for Jews.
This was-- this was a prisoner internment-- internment camp.
But by the time we get there, there was nobody there but Jews.
So I was there for a period of approximately over a month.
Way over a month.
Because by-- by knowing what's happening, well, probably closer to two months, five or six weeks before, once again, we were able to let dad-- mom and dad know that we are there.
And that's the place when probably the-- the service or the-- the-- the position, what my dad had in the Hungarian Army, served.
Because he found an officer, an army officer who was willing-- and we found out, naturally for a lot of money, he was willing to risk and come.
And suddenly there was one of the-- one of the prison guards came and looked for the two Gottliebs, Louis and George Gottleib.
We had no idea where, what and how.
Suddenly this Hungarian officer signed something.
And the two of us left with the officer, who never said two words to us, not two words.
He-- where we are going, what we are doing.
We were out of that camp in his car.
It was a rickety old car.
And drove us to Budapest.
Naturally, we knew right away, once we are in Budapest, where we are.
But we still had no idea what's going to happen to us.
And suddenly, he stopped right in the middle of-- that was a big square in front of the main railroad station.
And he stopped there.
And we were sitting in the car.
He got out.
And suddenly we saw, through our grocery store, through our-- our-- our store, we had a trucker who was an independent Christian, living the same time as Bicske, suddenly we saw his truck.
And it was full of furniture.
And we had no idea if this is a coincidence or what happened.
And suddenly he opened up the door.
And with a little kick in our behinds, kicked us towards the truck driver, who we knew from-- Bert.
I mean, he was continually in our house.
And he grabbed us and pushed us, both of us, in the back of the truck, into one of the piece of furniture, which was an armoire.
And he told us that I-- he doesn't want to hear a word.
And he doesn't want to hear a noise.
And he took off.
We trusted the man.
But we still had no idea.
And especially you are sitting in that armoire, dark.
And the truck is going, and--
He took us home.
Indescribable.
No word can describe it.
No word can describe that.
We thought-- mom and dad thought that we are gone.
God knows what's going to happen.
And I mean, not only emotional, but also very difficult.
I think it was quite difficult for my parents.
Because there had to be-- I know it by our action-- a huge change in our attitude.
I mean both my brother and I, in attitudes.
We were defiant.
We were not willing to take the orders from the SS or the Hungarian gendarme.
We were telling openly to mom and dad, that we want to run away and we want to hide.
And we have to-- we have to go to be a partisan.
We don't care what, but we don't want to be part of-- of being the sheep.
Very, very difficult.
Understanding, but-- but with the loving care to say that we, as a family, we stay together.
And there is no rhyme and reason why you have to be afraid.
Nothing is going to happen.
And nothing is going to happen to us.
From there?
And we were home-- memory wise, I think, so the fourth day or the fifth day, suddenly the ghetto started.
The law came out that all Jews have to be going to the certain area of that-- even of that small town where we lived, out of our house.
Our house was, basically, a first of all, a middle class or upper-middle-class house, and desirable probably, for them, to seize whatever they had in there.
And we moved into a house where each family received a room.
And that room, we were five.
But it be 7 or 8 or 3 or 20, one room per family.
Mom, dad, my brother and my grandfather.
And we were in that room, especially that house-- housing-- didn't even had-- I know it for a fact that it didn't have inside plumbing.
So--
One suitcase per person, we were allowed to take, period.
So whatever mom packed.
And I would say that by that time, the-- the-- the blase attitude of my brother and I was that we don't need anything.
Just let us go.
Especially once we get to the local ghetto.
I mean, we knew that the area, we knew people around there.
We knew where we could go or we wanted to go.
And our aim was, first of all, to go back to Budapest, where you can get lost in the crowd, where we knew an awful lot of people.
Especially through our sport, both of us, we knew.
And we had-- we were confident.
From there, from Bicske, we were taken within four days or five days to a huge old Hungarian military barracks, or military compound, in Komarom.
Komarom is K-O-M-A-R-O-M.
That city is smack in the middle of between Budapest and Vienna.
And--
Well, again, the whole town, including mom, dad, grandpa.
And there we had, already, uncles and aunts from other towns and and from all over which was a total, total disaster.
Because there we had the big barn.
That was our-- our place, where we were.
It's a big, huge barn, straw on the-- on the ground.
And-- and absolutely nothing.
I mean, they started to give us some food.
And they-- they basically took my brother and I to-- with a pushcart to carry some food in and out, because we were younger.
Must have been thousands and thousands of people.
It was a big, big, huge military compound.
Totally enclosed.
Totally-- totally guarded, and total-- totally walled.
Hungarian police, with a very few, but they had SS.
I guess they were the supervisors.
But it was Hungarian police.
No.
No, no, no.
We are not allowed to get-- step out of the-- out of the-- out of the ghetto.
I mean, that was-- that was probably the first time that you felt a total-- I mean, there was no sanitary waste.
There is just-- we had-- we had to go and pump up water from a-- from a well.
And it was-- for the first time, you felt like a third-class citizen who really doesn't belong.
Dehumanizing it as much as possible.
The food basically came in on a pushcart from the front door, from the front gate.
Well, military handed over the-- well, the-- these pushcarts to kids, like my brother and I, or the younger generation, who were stronger.
And basically, we had to bring it to different barracks and different-- not even barracks, buildings.
And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --that was the distribution.
It was-- it was-- it was not superbly organized.
Jews in Hungary were not organized the same way as we call it here.
Like I mentioned it, you mean, my-- my grandfather was the President of the synagogue.
My father was the President the school board.
So they tried to comfort our group.
But there wasn't a general major group who-- who even had the right-- first of all, nobody had any idea.
The shock was tremendous.
Nobody, really, idea what hit us, what's really happening.
Once again, there was no question that we are going to go.
The younger, able people, they are going to go and work in labor camps.
Work maybe as digging trenches, maybe go all the way up to the front.
But these were rumors.
Nobody had an idea.
It was a shock.
Shamefully, I have to say it, in 1944, this was already 1944, end of May.
We never heard of, we never knew that there is such a thing as a concentration camp, as an extermination camp, where Jews are being destroyed by-- Jews are being killed.
We lived in a bubble.
I mean we lived in a-- in a-- in a total, total ignorance.
And the only thing, what we could-- could visualize, that Hungarians are not going to allow anything-- anything worse then.
So we go to a labor camp.
And I remember at that time, already, dad was telling us that both of us, thank goodness.
And you see that the education and the upbringing, what you get, that we had to work.
We had to-- I mean, including physical work in our store, we had to do, both of us.
Especially summertime.
We were like-- like any other employee who had to carry the 50 or 75-pound bag on their shoulder like anybody else.
That thank goodness you're in good physical and strength.
And don't worry about it.
You will be able to work like anybody else, like I am.
Well, dad was saying it.
And grandpa will stay with mom.
That was the-- that was the worst, what we could-- what we could visualize. till it came the day, probably it was beginning of May.
Suddenly they herded us up to the railroad station, where we were shoved into railroad cars, to cattle cars.
My-- the whole family of mine and an awful lot of-- I mean, including a couple of uncles and aunts and cousins and friends.
And basically, our very close-- I knew everybody at that train, everybody in that car.
I mean, there wasn't a stranger in that car who we didn't know.
And that was the first time when I felt what human dignity is.
I mean, you know, you are 15 and a 1/2.
My brother was almost 18.
You are a brash, young, Mr.
Know-it-all.
Normal.
I think so it's normal, after my three kids.
And from there on they closed the door.
As they close the door, that they put in a pail of water, one pail of water.
I don't have to tell you that after going and marching, and everybody carrying-- that once again, the order was the one suitcase, you were allowed to carry.
I was able to carry, with my brother, mom's suitcase.
But I can't remember who it was carrying mom's and who was carrying grandpa's.
Because both of us were strong, I carried my own.
And naturally, we could carry mom-- dad carried his own.
And we get on the train.
And suddenly, that pail of water I have never seen one person drinking, everybody dipped their hand in it and wiped their mouth and probably their tongue, a little bit, a drop or two of water.
And that pail of water went around.
Everybody had a chance to do it.
But then that pail of water, by the evening-- this was early in the morning when we went, or before noon-- by the evening became the only possibility of having-- going to the bathroom.
And the-- the-- I mean, the humility suddenly, when you see people whom you-- not only that you know, but you respect, that it should be your mother's age, your father, your grandparents' age.
Those people were sheltered by a coat or by something.
And suddenly, they had to go and-- and do what-- well, the minimum at least, we knew.
And we grew up, and every human being in the normal condition knows that the minimum personal place where you can be.

And then well, being probably one of the two youngest, my brother volunteered that him and me, we were the one who emptied that pail, through that little hole under-- through that little window in the car.
And that was a horrifying first time, really, feeling.
That this is not life.
This-- this is humiliation to the end.
And you know, that this was a very, very difficult time in that train.
